responses.response: This argument targets the core vulnerability in our conditional support for S3. We do not treat it as a mere objection to overcome — we co-raised it, and it is settled evidence. The shortage of maths and science teachers is structural, and de-tracked heterogeneous classrooms demand more skilled, more experienced teachers, not fewer. The force of the objection is that even a fully-costed training-and-diagnostic bundle cannot conjure teachers who do not exist, so sequencing training before rollout (our condition f-h) is necessary but not sufficient: if the pipeline is empty, there is no one to train up in time. We accept this sharpens our position rather than defeats it. Our stance on S3 was already conditional and named teacher supply as condition (c) — an explicit precondition, not an afterthought. What the argument adds is that we cannot leave supply as one condition among several; it must be a hard gate. We therefore tighten: no phased rollout, not even a pilot, in any district until a funded, measured recruitment-and-retention trajectory (competitive salary, working-condition improvements, training uptake) shows the local teacher supply can staff de-tracked cohorts without degrading load. This is a revision in stringency, not in direction — our documented segregation-reduction commitment stands, but we bind it to a demonstrated staffing floor rather than a promised one.
responses.new_condition: Support for S3 (and any pilot of it) is withheld until a fully-funded teacher recruitment-and-retention trajectory demonstrates, with measured local staffing data, that de-tracked cohorts in the pilot districts can be staffed without increasing per-teacher load — teacher-supply adequacy becomes a hard precondition gate, not one condition among several, and pilot commencement is contingent on meeting a defined staffing floor.
